text: The Nathanson liver retractor elevates the left lobe of the liver. A 36 French gastric bougie is positioned towards the lesser curvature. The surgeon begins dissecting the short gastric vessels using a harmonic scalpel, with the left hand holding a Deaver atraumatic retractor and the right hand elevating the omentum to expose the dissection plane. The separation of the greater omentum from the stomach is initiated.

Although the sleeve gastrectomy is equally easy with the laparoscopic surgery, but in this patient, patient has opted for da Vinci robotic sleeve gastrectomy. And after that, colicistectomy also will be performed in the same patient.
The surgeon uses a suction device to aspirate fumes generated by the harmonic scalpel during dissection, ensuring a clear field of vision.
The spleen is visible in the surgical field, with the optical port positioned supraumbilically to provide a clear view of the area.

The surgeon carefully separates posterior adhesions using a harmonic scalpel, maintaining a safe distance from the spleen to prevent bleeding. The adhesions are gently detached, ensuring the tip of the scalpel remains away from the spleen.

The surgeon reaches the left crus, separating the peritoneum in this area. The posterior wall is examined for adhesions, and dissection continues towards the pylorus, ensuring a distance of six to eight centimeters through the antrum.
The surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing posterior wall adhesions that are visible and need to be addressed during the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon safely separates the posterior wall adhesions using dissection techniques, while retracting the bougie to prevent inappropriate traction during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining adhesions near the pylorus. The bougie is pushed down towards the pylorus, positioned close to the lesser curvature, while instruments are used to guide its placement. An upper GI endoscope may be utilized for further visualization.

The indentation from the bougie is visible, directed towards the pylorus and positioned near the lesser curvature. The surgeon begins stapling the stomach for sleeve gastrectomy, using either a Tri-Stapler or Ethicon Echelon device. If bleeding occurs, a titanium clip may be applied to control it. The surgeon may choose to clamp the staple and wait before firing, or fire and keep the stapler pressed before disengaging.

The surgeon uses suction to check for any bleeding before continuing with stapling the stomach. A robotic or laparoscopic stapler is used, with the assistant potentially firing the stapler. The posterior wall is examined, and stapling continues to create the sleeve gastrectomy, typically requiring six or seven cartridges.

The surgeon approaches the gastroesophageal junction, maintaining a safe distance from the esophagus to avoid creating 'dog ears,' which are prone to leaks due to reduced blood supply. The procedure concludes with the separation of any remaining adhesions on the posterior wall using harmonic dissection.

The surgeon checks for bleeding along the stapler line and begins continuous suturing using a barb suture, such as V-lock, to reinforce the staple line. The seromuscular layer of both the posterior and anterior walls is incorporated, burying the staple margin within the seromuscular layer of the stomach. The robotic system facilitates precise suturing, which can be challenging in laparoscopic procedures due to needle handling and manipulation.

The surgeon applies traction to the stomach using sutures to visualize the posterior wall. Continuous suturing is performed, ensuring that vessels are not inadvertently pierced to avoid hematoma formation. A titanium clip is applied to control minor oozing, and the suturing process continues.

The surgeon cuts the barb suture without needing a termination knot, as it holds on its own. The needle is removed, and the procedure transitions to the cholecystectomy part. The patient has a fatty liver with an enlarged left lobe, making ergonomic access challenging due to the proximity of the liver to the costal margins. Despite the difficulty in retracting the liver, the robotic instruments allow for continued manipulation. Minimal adhesions are visible with the gallbladder, and the surgeon begins separating these adhesions.

The surgeon attempts to maintain proximity to the gallbladder during dissection. The fundus traction is limited as the fundus clasp cannot be pushed towards the diaphragm. The majority of traction is applied to the Hartmann's pouch using the left da Vinci robotic grasper.

The surgeon performs posterior peritoneal dissection while applying anterolateral traction to expose the cystic duct. The anterior peritoneum remains attached, requiring separation to achieve a critical view of safety. The cystic duct and artery are clearly defined within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the posterior and anterior windows, with a visible lymph node aiding in the recognition of the artery. Adhesions are separated, revealing a clear view of Calot's triangle, including the cystic duct and artery. A titanium medium large size clip is applied to the artery, with harmonic device used for additional hemostasis.

The surgeon applies three titanium clips on the cystic duct, with precise spacing between them, before cutting the duct using scissors. The lumen of the duct is clearly visible during the cutting process. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver, with bleeding checked and further dissection initiated.

The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to define the areolar plane for dissection, gradually separating the gallbladder from the liver. Due to limited space between the liver and costal margins, traction is directed inferiorly, occasionally approaching the fundus to pull downward.

The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy and checks for any bleeding, using suction as needed. An endo bag is prepared to remove both the stomach and gallbladder together. Methylene blue is injected to inspect the staple line, and the separated stomach and gallbladder are placed into the wide-open endo bag for removal.

The surgeon uses a claw forceps to hold the neck of the gallbladder, ensuring smooth extraction through a 15 mm or 12 mm port. The endo bag containing the specimen is retracted through the port. After removal, the ports are closed under vision. An upper GI endoscope is introduced to inspect the stapler line margins, and methylene blue is injected for further inspection.
